text: This video presents a laparoscopic redo of a gastrointestinal adrenostomy in a patient that developed a stricture after a gastric bypass for morbid obesity.
The main complaints were nausea and vomiting, intractable to endoscopic dilatations.
The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity and carefully dissects adhesions between the gastric remnant and the major omentum, exposing the short gastric vessels.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the short gastric vessels, mobilizing the stomach up to the gastroesophageal junction, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
In cases where redo gastrodegenosomies are performed in order to better rule our gastrogastric fistulas, we routinely perform this kind of mobilization.
The surgeon clearly dissects the gastric remnant, the pouch, and the alimentary limb, visually distinguishing these anatomical structures during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon identifies the area of stenosis and proceeds to transect the distal part of the gastric remnant, separating it from the gastrointestinal tract.
The surgeon elevates the gastric remnant after completing the dissection and mobilization of surrounding structures.
The surgeon ensures the left gastric artery remains intact to maintain blood supply to the gastric pouch, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect adhesions from the gastric remnant, progressing towards the gastrojejunostomy. The gastrojejunostomy is clearly identified and divided from the distal part of the remnant.
The gastric remnant is visualized in conjunction with the surrounding structures, appearing as a distinct block within the surgical field.
The surgeon gains control of the gastric remnant and identifies the left gastric artery on the pouch, ensuring its viability for blood supply.
The surgeon passes the UL-II instrument and identifies the gastroesophageal junction, focusing on its anatomical location.
The surgeon transects the gastric pouch proximal to the stenotic and penetrated gastro-degenerostomy, ensuring clear separation from the affected area.
The surgeon uses green cartridge linear staplers to perform a stapling action, typically employed in redo cases, on the visible tissue structures.
since the gastric tissue is thickened due to the previous surgeries and chronic inflammatory process.
The surgeon lyses adhesions between the alimentary limb and the mesenterium, preparing to reposition the limb to the upper abdomen in an anti-colic, anti-gastric manner without tension.
The surgeon oversews the staple lines, reinforcing the surgical connections to ensure stability and prevent leakage.
The surgeon runs vial sutures along the surgical site, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon performs the same procedure at the level of the gastric stump, likely involving the use of green cartridge linear staplers to manage thickened gastric tissue.
These patients are placed on proton pump inhibitors since a retained antrum might be the source of a recurrent marginal ulceration.

The surgeon reinforces the staple lines and proceeds to reconstruct the upper portion of the gastrointestinal tract by performing a side-to-side gastroenterostomy, aligning the stomach and jejunum for anastomosis.
The surgeon uses clip-up pliers to enhance hemostasis, securing vessels and minimizing bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs oversawing on both sides of the gastric remnant and gastric pouch, addressing dilatations visible in the surgical field.
that gastro-degenerostomonic strictures do not subside after three to four dilatations.
The surgeon performs an enterotomy at the midpoint between the mesenteric and anti-mesenteric borders on the small bowel, creating an opening in the intestinal wall.
The surgeon uses a blue cartridge linear stapler to perform a side-to-side gastroenterostomy on the posterior wall, aligning the gastric pouch with the jejunum.
The surgeon performs a gastrotomy, creating an incision in the stomach wall.
The surgeon uses an E-wall tube to assist in the maneuver, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the posterior wall of the gastrojejunostomy using a linear stapler, securing the connection between the stomach and jejunum.
The surgeon completes the anterior wall of the gastrojejunostomy using a double layer of rendezvous sutures, securing the connection between the stomach and jejunum.
We perform routine intraoperative EGDs.
And since this is a training program as well, we also teach how to do leak tests by means of air leak and methylene blue.
The surgeon applies a full thickness running suture to the surgical site, ensuring secure closure of the tissue layers.
The surgeon runs the suture in both directions, from proximal to distal and then back, locking the most distal part of the suture to the proximal part.
The surgeon places surgical drains in the abdominal cavity, a routine step in both primary and reoperative procedures.
While in primary procedures, drains are removed before patients are discharged home.
In performing reoperative surgery, the drains are left in place for at least ten to fourteen days.
